script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'bb-multi-repo-audit',
  description: 'Comprehensive bug-bounty security audit across 5 cloned targets: fan-out deep-read finders, adversarial 3-lens verification, ranked synthesis',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Find', detail: 'deep-read finders per (target, vuln-class) cluster' },
    { title: 'Verify', detail: 'adversarial refute-by-default panel per candidate finding' },
    { title: 'Synthesize', detail: 'dedup, rank by severity x exploitability x payout' },
  ],
}

const TARGETS = `C:/bugBounty/targets`

const FINDINGS_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    findings: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object',
        additionalProperties: false,
        properties: {
          title: { type: 'string' },
          target: { type: 'string' },
          file: { type: 'string', description: 'repo-relative path' },
          line: { type: 'integer' },
          vuln_class: { type: 'string' },
          cwe: { type: 'string' },
          severity: { type: 'string', enum: ['Critical', 'High', 'Medium', 'Low', 'Info'] },
          source: { type: 'string', description: 'where attacker-controlled input enters' },
          sink: { type: 'string', description: 'dangerous operation reached' },
          data_flow: { type: 'string', description: 'concrete path source->sink, with function names' },
          preconditions: { type: 'string' },
          exploitability: { type: 'string' },
          code_excerpt: { type: 'string' },
          confidence: { type: 'number' },
        },
        required: ['title', 'target', 'file', 'vuln_class', 'severity', 'source', 'sink', 'data_flow', 'confidence'],
      },
    },
    coverage_notes: { type: 'string', description: 'what you read and what you could not rule out' },
  },
  required: ['findings'],
}

const VERDICT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    is_real: { type: 'boolean' },
    refuted: { type: 'boolean' },
    reason: { type: 'string' },
    attacker_controlled: { type: 'boolean' },
    sink_reachable: { type: 'boolean' },
    mitigation_present: { type: 'boolean' },
    adjusted_severity: { type: 'string' },
    exploit_sketch: { type: 'string' },
    confidence: { type: 'number' },
  },
  required: ['is_real', 'refuted', 'reason', 'confidence'],
}

const REPORT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    executive_summary: { type: 'string' },
    ranked_findings: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object',
        additionalProperties: false,
        properties: {
          rank: { type: 'integer' },
          title: { type: 'string' },
          target: { type: 'string' },
          file: { type: 'string' },
          line: { type: 'integer' },
          vuln_class: { type: 'string' },
          cwe: { type: 'string' },
          severity: { type: 'string' },
          cvss_vector: { type: 'string' },
          why_high_value: { type: 'string' },
          exploitability: { type: 'string' },
          program_payout_note: { type: 'string' },
          suggested_next_step: { type: 'string' },
          confidence: { type: 'number' },
        },
        required: ['rank', 'title', 'target', 'severity', 'why_high_value', 'suggested_next_step'],
      },
    },
    program_scope_notes: { type: 'string' },
    recommended_priority_order: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
  },
  required: ['executive_summary', 'ranked_findings', 'program_scope_notes'],
}

const CLUSTERS = [
  // ---- Weblate (Django web app; large attack surface) ----
  { key: 'web-gitexport', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate/gitexport`,
    threat: 'Web-reachable git smart-HTTP export endpoint. Attackers: anonymous or low-priv authenticated web users sending crafted HTTP requests (path, query, service params). Goal classes: command injection into git http-backend/subprocess, path traversal to read arbitrary repos/files, authz bypass to clone private project repos, info disclosure of credentials in remote URLs.',
    classes: 'OS command injection, path traversal, broken access control (private repo exposure), credential leakage' },
  { key: 'web-vcs', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate/vcs`,
    threat: 'VCS layer that runs git/hg/ssh/gpg subprocesses with arguments derived from project configuration (repo URL, branch, push URL, credentials) set by project admins (a lower-privilege role than superuser) and sometimes from remote repo content. Watch for argument injection (values beginning with "-" or "--", git "ext::"/"fd::" transports, "file://"), SSRF via remote URL fetch, and credential exposure in process args/logs.',
    classes: 'OS command / argument injection, SSRF, credential exposure' },
  { key: 'web-ssrf-mt', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate/machinery`,
    threat: 'Machine-translation provider backends issue outbound HTTP requests. Some providers allow an admin/user-configured base URL or endpoint. Also inspect weblate/accounts/avatar.py and any requests/urlopen with user/component-influenced URLs across the repo. Goal: SSRF to cloud metadata (169.254.169.254) or internal services, blind SSRF, request smuggling.',
    classes: 'SSRF, outbound request forgery, DNS rebinding' },
  { key: 'web-ssti-xss', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate`,
    threat: 'Server-side template injection and stored XSS. Focus: utils/render.py (Django Template render of user-provided templates such as commit-message/addon templates), utils/markdown.py, utils/html.py, trans/templatetags/translations.py, mark_safe/format_html sites, addon configuration. Translators/project-admins supply template strings and translation content rendered into pages or commit messages.',
    classes: 'SSTI (Django template injection), stored/reflected XSS, HTML sanitizer bypass' },
  { key: 'web-sqli', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate`,
    threat: 'SQL injection via raw SQL. Find cursor.execute, .raw(, RawSQL, QuerySet.extra(, and string-formatted SQL where any fragment is user-influenced (search queries, filters, language/locale codes, ordering params). Focus utils/db.py, lang/models.py, auth/, memory/, trans/models, api filtering.',
    classes: 'SQL injection' },
  { key: 'web-authz-api', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate`,
    threat: 'Broken access control / IDOR / privilege escalation in the REST API and views. Focus api/views.py, api/permissions.py, auth/, accounts/views.py, trans/views/*. Look for object-level authz gaps (acting on objects by id without ownership/permission check), mass assignment, permission checks that trust client input, and unauthenticated state-changing endpoints.',
    classes: 'IDOR, broken access control, privilege escalation, mass assignment, CSRF' },
  { key: 'web-fileops', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate`,
    threat: 'File upload / archive extraction / path construction. Focus trans/views/files.py (upload), screenshots/ (image upload), formats/ (parsing uploaded translation files), addons file writes, any zipfile/tarfile extraction (zip-slip), and os.path.join with user filenames. Translators upload files; goal is path traversal, zip-slip arbitrary write, arbitrary file read.',
    classes: 'path traversal, zip-slip / arbitrary file write, arbitrary file read, unrestricted upload' },
  { key: 'web-deser-rce', target: 'weblate', base: `${TARGETS}/weblate/weblate`,
    threat: 'Insecure deserialization and code execution. Find yaml.load without SafeLoader (settings_docker.py uses it), pickle/marshal of untrusted data (cache, sessions, celery payloads), eval/exec, and addon/automation hooks that execute user-influenced code or scripts. Also check celery task argument trust boundaries.',
    classes: 'insecure deserialization (RCE), eval/exec injection, unsafe YAML' },

  // ---- Chia (P2P blockchain node; untrusted-peer network surface) ----
  { key: 'chia-daemon', target: 'chia-blockchain', base: `${TARGETS}/chia-blockchain/chia/daemon`,
    threat: 'The daemon exposes a local WebSocket control server (server.py) plus keychain_server.py/keychain_proxy.py. Threat: a process or web page able to reach the daemon websocket, or weak auth, leading to command/argument injection when launching plotters/services (subprocess), arbitrary command exec, path traversal in file ops, or keychain access without authorization. Check websocket origin/auth validation and how command names/args from messages reach subprocess.',
    classes: 'command/argument injection, missing/weak daemon auth, keychain access bypass, path traversal' },
  { key: 'chia-p2p-tls', target: 'chia-blockchain', base: `${TARGETS}/chia-blockchain/chia/server`,
    threat: 'Untrusted P2P peers connect over TLS. Focus ssl_context.py (cert generation/validation, allow-any-cert vs CA pinning), ws_connection.py (message framing/size limits), node_discovery.py + address_manager.py (peer address handling), upnp.py (UPnP), rate_limits*.py. Threat: a malicious peer. Goal classes: TLS/cert validation weakness or MITM, remote DoS (unbounded allocation, decompression, parsing), SSRF/abuse via UPnP or peer-supplied addresses.',
    classes: 'improper certificate validation / MITM, remote DoS, peer-data injection' },
  { key: 'chia-rpc', target: 'chia-blockchain', base: `${TARGETS}/chia-blockchain/chia/rpc`,
    threat: 'RPC server (rpc_server.py) and clients. Threat: a client able to reach the RPC port (sometimes bound beyond localhost, or reachable cross-origin via the websocket). Look at auth/origin checks, whether RPC handlers validate parameters, SSRF in any handler that fetches URLs, and exposure of keys/secrets through RPC responses.',
    classes: 'missing RPC auth, SSRF, parameter injection, secret/key disclosure' },
  { key: 'chia-keys', target: 'chia-blockchain', base: `${TARGETS}/chia-blockchain/chia/cmds`,
    threat: 'Key/seed/signing handling and CLI subprocess use. Focus signer.py, keys_funcs.py, start_funcs.py, init_funcs.py, plotters. Goal: private key / mnemonic exposure (logs, world-readable files, insecure temp), insecure randomness for key material, command/argument injection in start/plotter subprocess calls.',
    classes: 'key/secret exposure, insecure file permissions, weak randomness, command injection' },

  // ---- Coinbase (client SDK; MITM / malicious-server / local threat) ----
  { key: 'cb-sdk', target: 'coinbase-advanced-py', base: `${TARGETS}/coinbase-advanced-py/coinbase`,
    threat: 'Official Coinbase REST/WebSocket SDK that runs in the API consumer process and authenticates with API keys (JWT). Known issue to CONFIRM (not re-report as novel): bare ssl.SSLContext() in websocket/websocket_base.py disables cert verification (CWE-295). Also scrutinize jwt_generator.py (nonce/exp handling, alg confusion, key handling), api_base.py / rest/rest_base.py (TLS verify on requests, secret logging, error leakage), and any secret written to logs.',
    classes: 'improper certificate validation, JWT signing weakness, secret logging/exposure, TLS verify disabled' },
]

function finderPrompt(c) {
  return [
    `You are a senior application-security auditor performing a RESPONSIBLE-DISCLOSURE bug-bounty review of an open-source project. Be rigorous and skeptical; only report issues you can defend with a concrete data-flow.`,
    ``,
    `TARGET: ${c.target}`,
    `PRIMARY DIRECTORY (read every relevant .py here; recurse into subdirs): ${c.base}`,
    `You also have the whole repo at ${TARGETS}/${c.target} — Grep/Glob to follow call chains, base classes, callers, settings/defaults, and URL routing. READ FULL FILES, not just grep hits.`,
    ``,
    `THREAT MODEL FOR THIS CLUSTER:`,
    c.threat,
    ``,
    `VULNERABILITY CLASSES TO HUNT: ${c.classes}`,
    ``,
    `METHOD:`,
    `1. Identify the dangerous sinks in scope (subprocess/os calls, SQL execution, deserialization, template render, file path use, outbound HTTP, TLS context, crypto/key use, authz checks).`,
    `2. For each, trace BACKWARD to find whether an attacker-controlled source (per the threat model above) can reach it, naming the functions/handlers/routes on the path.`,
    `3. Check for mitigations on the path (parameterization, shlex/list-form subprocess, escaping/autoescape, allow-lists, permission decorators, size limits). If an effective mitigation exists, do NOT report it.`,
    `4. Determine reachability and required privilege. Anonymous/low-priv remote = highest value.`,
    ``,
    `RULES:`,
    `- Quality over quantity. Report at most 6 findings, ranked best-first. Zero findings is an acceptable and honest answer.`,
    `- Every finding needs: exact repo-relative file path, line number, the source, the sink, and the concrete source->sink data_flow with function names.`,
    `- Assign severity by realistic impact AND reachability (Critical/High only for remotely or low-priv reachable serious impact).`,
    `- Set confidence in [0,1]; be honest. Do not pad with theoretical/defense-in-depth nits.`,
    `Return ONLY the structured object.`,
  ].join('\n')
}

function verifyPrompt(f, lens, c) {
  const common = [
    `You are an adversarial security reviewer. Your job is to REFUTE the following candidate vulnerability. Default to refuted=true unless the evidence is solid. Read the actual code at the cited path before deciding.`,
    ``,
    `TARGET: ${f.target}  (repo root: ${TARGETS}/${f.target})`,
    `THREAT MODEL: ${c.threat}`,
    ``,
    `CANDIDATE FINDING:`,
    `  title: ${f.title}`,
    `  class: ${f.vuln_class} (${f.cwe || 'cwe?'})`,
    `  severity claimed: ${f.severity}`,
    `  file: ${f.file}:${f.line || '?'}`,
    `  source: ${f.source}`,
    `  sink: ${f.sink}`,
    `  data_flow: ${f.data_flow}`,
    `  excerpt: ${f.code_excerpt || '(none)'}`,
    ``,
  ]
  const lensText = {
    reachability: `LENS = REACHABILITY & CONTROL. Open the cited file and trace the path. Is the "source" genuinely attacker-controlled at the claimed privilege level in a real default deployment? Is the sink actually reached (not dead code, not gated behind admin-only/superuser, not disabled by default config)? Verify routing/registration. If the source is actually trusted (e.g., already-superuser, server-operator config not exposed to attackers) or the path is unreachable, set refuted=true.`,
    sanitization: `LENS = MITIGATION & SANITIZATION. Look specifically for defenses between source and sink: ORM parameterization, shlex.quote or list-form subprocess without shell=True, Django autoescape, HTML sanitizer allow-lists, URL/scheme allow-lists, SafeLoader, permission decorators, path normalization with containment checks, size/recursion limits. If an effective mitigation defeats the exploit, set refuted=true.`,
    repro: `LENS = CONCRETE EXPLOIT. Construct the precise input/request/payload that triggers the issue and state the resulting impact. If you cannot produce a plausible working exploit from the actual code (or it requires unrealistic preconditions), set refuted=true.`,
  }[lens]
  return [
    ...common,
    lensText,
    ``,
    `Set is_real=true ONLY if, after reading the code, the finding survives your lens. Provide reason and, if real, an exploit_sketch and adjusted_severity. Return ONLY the structured object.`,
  ].join('\n')
}

// ---------- FIND -> VERIFY pipeline (verify each cluster's findings as soon as it returns) ----------
const clusterResults = await pipeline(
  CLUSTERS,
  (c) => agent(finderPrompt(c), { label: `find:${c.key}`, phase: 'Find', schema: FINDINGS_SCHEMA }),
  (review, c) => {
    const findings = (review && review.findings) ? review.findings : []
    return parallel(findings.map((f) => () => {
      const heavy = f.severity === 'Critical' || f.severity === 'High'
      const lenses = heavy ? ['reachability', 'sanitization', 'repro'] : ['repro']
      return parallel(lenses.map((lens) => () =>
        agent(verifyPrompt(f, lens, c), { label: `verify:${c.key}:${lens}`, phase: 'Verify', schema: VERDICT_SCHEMA })
      )).then((votes) => {
        const v = votes.filter(Boolean)
        const real = v.filter((x) => x.is_real && !x.refuted).length
        const survives = v.length > 0 && real >= Math.ceil(v.length / 2)
        return { ...f, cluster: c.key, verifiers: v, votes_real: real, votes_total: v.length, survives }
      })
    }))
  }
)

const allCandidates = clusterResults.flat().filter(Boolean)
const confirmed = allCandidates.filter((f) => f.survives)
log(`Candidates: ${allCandidates.length} | survived verification: ${confirmed.length}`)

// ---------- SYNTHESIZE ----------
phase('Synthesize')
const synthInput = confirmed.map((f) => ({
  title: f.title, target: f.target, file: f.file, line: f.line, vuln_class: f.vuln_class,
  cwe: f.cwe, severity: f.severity, source: f.source, sink: f.sink, data_flow: f.data_flow,
  exploitability: f.exploitability, finder_confidence: f.confidence,
  votes: `${f.votes_real}/${f.votes_total}`,
  verifier_notes: (f.verifiers || []).map((v) => ({ real: v.is_real, refuted: v.refuted, sev: v.adjusted_severity, sketch: v.exploit_sketch, reason: v.reason })),
}))

const report = await agent([
  `You are the lead of a bug-bounty team. Below is JSON of security findings that already survived an adversarial 3-lens verification panel. Produce a prioritized, disclosure-ready report aimed at earning the highest-value bounties.`,
  ``,
  `INSTRUCTIONS:`,
  `- DEDUP / context: the Coinbase bare ssl.SSLContext() issue (CWE-295, websocket_base.py ~line 150) is ALREADY catalogued in our store as PY-SSL-001 and not yet submitted; if present, mark it "already-tracked" rather than novel, but keep it in the ranking.`,
  `- Rank by (severity x exploitability x program payout potential). Assign a CVSS:3.1 vector and confirm the severity bucket for each.`,
  `- Use web search to assess which of these projects run a PAYING bug-bounty program and rough payout tiers: Coinbase (HackerOne), Chia Network, Weblate. Put conclusions in program_scope_notes. If a tool is unavailable, reason from prior knowledge and say so.`,
  `- Be conservative and defensible: drop anything that reads as theoretical. It is fine to return few findings.`,
  `- For each finding give why_high_value, a concrete suggested_next_step (e.g., "write PoC X", "confirm scope on program Y", "add scanner rule Z"), and program_payout_note.`,
  `- recommended_priority_order: ordered list of finding titles for submission.`,
  ``,
  `FINDINGS JSON:`,
  JSON.stringify(synthInput, null, 2),
].join('\n'), { label: 'synthesize', phase: 'Synthesize', schema: REPORT_SCHEMA })

return {
  candidate_count: allCandidates.length,
  confirmed_count: confirmed.length,
  confirmed,
  report,
}

confirmed.title: Authenticated push URL containing credentials written to logs at INFO level
confirmed.source: GitMergeRequestBase.configure_fork_remote() builds push_url via authenticate_url(http_url, credentials), embedding the configured username and API token as 'username:token@host'. Token originates from the *_CREDENTIALS settings (e.g. GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host]['token']).
confirmed.sink: self.log(f"Configuring fork remote '{remote_name}': {push_url}", level=logging.INFO) -> weblate.vcs logger -> log aggregation / Sentry breadcrumbs
confirmed.data_flow: get_credentials() reads token from settings -> push() -> fork()/configure_fork_remote(ssh_url, http_url, credentials) -> push_url = authenticate_url(http_url, credentials) (URL now contains plaintext token, git.py:1493-1502) -> self.log(... push_url, level=INFO) at git.py:1422. Emitted whenever a merge-request backend configures its fork remote during a push.
confirmed.exploitability: Requires read access to Weblate VCS logs/Sentry breadcrumbs. Impact is leakage of an org-scoped Git provider token to anyone with log access.
confirmed.preconditions: A GitHub/GitLab/Gitea merge-request VCS backend is configured with credentials and a push is performed.

verifiers.reason: Confirmed by reading the code. configure_fork_remote() at git.py:1413-1424 builds push_url via authenticate_url() (git.py:1493-1502), which embeds username:token@host using only urllib.parse.quote (URL-encoding, not redaction). The resulting URL still contains the plaintext API token. It is passed to self.log(f"Configuring fork remote '{remote_name}': {push_url}", level=logging.INFO). The base log() method (base.py:351-352) does NOT sanitize: it calls LOGGER.log(level, "%s: %s", cls._cmd, message) verbatim. sanitize_error_message (base.py:547-548) is a no-op and is only applied to error paths (base.py:502,527), never to log(). So the token reaches the weblate.vcs logger at INFO whenever a merge-request backend (GitHub/GitLab/Gitea/Pagure/Bitbucket) configures its fork remote during push, and the http push_scheme branch is taken. The token comes from *_CREDENTIALS settings (e.g. GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host]['token']). This is a legitimate CWE-532 credential-exposure / logging-hygiene defect. It is not attacker-controlled and not an injection; impact requires read access to logs / Sentry breadcrumbs where the secret is then exposed, so Low severity is accurate.
verifiers.exploit_sketch: Configure a component to push via a merge-request backend (e.g. GitHub) with GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host] = {username, token}, where the push_scheme resolves to http(s) rather than ssh. Trigger a push (Weblate auto-push or admin "Push" action). push() -> fork() -> configure_fork_remote(ssh_url, http_url, credentials) executes the else-branch (push_url = authenticate_url(http_url, credentials)), producing https://username:TOKEN@host/.... self.log(..., level=logging.INFO) emits this line to the weblate.vcs logger. Anyone with read access to application logs or Sentry breadcrumbs (ops staff, log-aggregation users, or an attacker who has compromised log storage) recovers the plaintext API token and can act as that user against the upstream Git host. No malicious input is required; the secret leak is the impact.

confirmed.title: Repository URL with embedded credentials logged to error-tracking breadcrumbs in command args
confirmed.source: component.repo / component.push set by project admins may contain embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:token@host/repo.git). For clone the URL is placed directly into the subprocess argv via GitRepository._clone (git.py:341-355).
confirmed.sink: Repository._popen builds text_cmd = ' '.join(args) and passes it to cls.add_breadcrumb(text_cmd, ...) at base.py:519-525, forwarding to Sentry/error breadcrumbs unsanitized.
confirmed.data_flow: admin sets component.repo with embedded password -> clone()/clone_from()/_clone() -> _popen(['git','clone','--', source, target]) -> text_cmd = ' '.join(args) (base.py:476) includes the full URL with credentials -> add_breadcrumb(text_cmd, ...). sanitize_error_message() is only applied to process output on failure (base.py:527), never to text_cmd, so the credential-bearing command string is recorded verbatim.
confirmed.exploitability: Needs access to breadcrumb/error-tracking data. Risk is leakage of repo credentials to a broader set of operators/Sentry viewers than intended.
confirmed.preconditions: A component repo/push URL embeds credentials in the URL and a git operation runs.

verifiers.reason: Verified at source. In _popen (base.py:476) text_cmd = " ".join(args) builds the full command string and is passed verbatim to cls.add_breadcrumb(text_cmd, ...) at base.py:519-525. For git, _clone (git.py:341-355) places the `source` URL directly into argv, so when an admin sets component.repo/push to a credentialed URL (https://user:token@host/repo.git — a Weblate-supported pattern), the embedded credentials appear in text_cmd. sanitize_error_message (base.py:547, the no-op default) is applied only to process output on a nonzero return code (base.py:527), never to text_cmd. add_breadcrumb forwards message+data to sentry_sdk.add_breadcrumb (errors.py:165-173), so the credential-bearing command string is recorded as a Sentry breadcrumb and attached to subsequent error events. This is a genuine CWE-532 credential-exposure-to-logging issue. It is gated by SENTRY_DSN being configured (no exposure otherwise), and the credentials are admin's own and only reach the deployment's own Sentry, which is why Low is appropriate — there is no cross-tenant or privilege-escalation impact.
verifiers.exploit_sketch: A project admin (lower-priv than superuser) configures a component with repo = https://gituser:ghp_SECRETTOKEN@github.com/org/repo.git. On clone, _popen runs ['git', <auth args>, 'clone', ..., '--', 'https://gituser:ghp_SECRETTOKEN@github.com/org/repo.git', target]; text_cmd joins these including the credentialed URL and is sent to add_breadcrumb. When any later VCS operation in that request/transaction errors and report_error fires to Sentry (SENTRY_DSN set), the breadcrumb trail — including the plaintext token — is uploaded to the Sentry project, visible to anyone with Sentry access (potentially broader than those entitled to the repo credential).

confirmed.title: WebSocket TLS certificate verification disabled via bare ssl.SSLContext()
confirmed.source: Network position: an active man-in-the-middle on the path between the SDK consumer process and advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com (malicious Wi-Fi/router, ARP/DNS spoof, compromised upstream proxy).
confirmed.sink: websockets.connect(..., ssl=ssl.SSLContext() ...) at websocket_base.py:150 establishing the WSS connection.
confirmed.data_flow: WSBase.open() -> _run_coroutine_threadsafe(open_async()) -> open_async() (websocket_base.py:129-151) calls websockets.connect with ssl=ssl.SSLContext() whenever base_url starts with 'wss://'. A bare ssl.SSLContext() constructor leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, so the server certificate and hostname are NOT validated. The authenticated JWT bearer token produced by _build_subscription_message() at websocket_base.py:561 via jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt(self.api_key, self.api_secret) is then transmitted over this unverified channel during subscribe_async() (await self.websocket.send at line 267). An active MITM can present any self-signed certificate, complete the handshake, capture the JWT, and observe/inject account and market stream data.
confirmed.preconditions: Attacker has active on-path/MITM capability. The default base_url is wss://, so the default configuration is affected. JWT is short-lived (120s), which bounds replay value but does not prevent live interception or tampering.
confirmed.exploitability: Requires an active on-path attacker; no special privilege within the SDK. Fix: use ssl.create_default_context() (CERT_REQUIRED + hostname check) or omit the ssl argument so websockets applies its secure default.

verifiers.reason: Confirmed at websocket_base.py:150. The default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com (WS_BASE_URL / WS_USER_BASE_URL), so the startswith("wss://") branch is taken on every default deployment, passing ssl=ssl.SSLContext(). A bare ssl.SSLContext() (no protocol arg) defaults to PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT in modern Python but the no-arg constructor leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, disabling both certificate and hostname validation. The sink is on the primary connection path (WSBase.open -> open_async), not dead code, not gated behind admin/superuser config, not disabled by default. The threat model is a network MITM (rogue Wi-Fi/router, ARP/DNS spoof, malicious proxy) on the path to the public Coinbase WS endpoint -- a genuine, attacker-controllable position requiring no privileges in the victim process. After connect, the authenticated JWT bearer (build_ws_jwt) is sent over this unverified channel during subscribe, so a MITM presenting a self-signed cert completes the handshake and captures the JWT plus account/market stream. Matches the documented known CWE-295 issue.
verifiers.exploit_sketch: Attacker on the same LAN/Wi-Fi as the SDK consumer performs ARP or DNS spoofing for advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com, terminates the WSS handshake with a self-signed cert. Because verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, the client accepts it. The attacker proxies (or drops) traffic, captures the JWT sent in the subscribe message, and can observe/inject account and market data frames.

An active MITM (malicious Wi-Fi/AP, ARP/DNS spoof, rogue proxy) on the path to advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com presents any self-signed certificate. Because websockets.connect is invoked with ssl=ssl.SSLContext() (a bare context whose CPython defaults are verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False), the TLS handshake completes without validating the cert chain or hostname. The default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com, so the vulnerable branch is the standard path. After connect, _build_subscription_message() (line ~561) calls jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt(api_key, api_secret) and subscribe_async() sends the authenticated JWT bearer token over the unverified channel (self.websocket.send). The attacker captures the JWT and can observe/inject account and market stream data.
verifiers.reason: Verified at websocket_base.py:150: ssl=ssl.SSLContext() if self.base_url.startswith("wss://") else None. A bare ssl.SSLContext() in CPython leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False — no certificate or hostname validation. The correct call would be ssl.create_default_context(). No mitigation exists between source and sink: there is no scheme allow-list that affects TLS, no cert pinning, no verify override, no load_default_certs, no SafeLoader-style defense relevant here. The default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com, so the vulnerable branch executes by default, and authenticated JWTs are subsequently transmitted over the unverified channel. The finding survives the mitigation lens. This is a known/confirmed issue (CWE-295), not a novel re-report; CVSS 7.5 (High) is consistent with the SDK's anchor vector.

Confirmed in source. coinbase/websocket/websocket_base.py:150 passes ssl=ssl.SSLContext() to websockets.connect whenever base_url starts with wss://, which is the default (constants.py: WS_BASE_URL = "wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com", used as the default base_url in __init__). A default ssl.SSLContext() leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, so neither the server certificate chain nor the hostname is validated. The authenticated JWT bearer (jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt over api_key/api_secret) is then sent over this unverified channel during subscribe. This is a genuine CWE-295 instance and matches the project's already-documented confirmed finding. The only realistic precondition is an active MITM network position, which is exactly the standard threat model for this weakness and is reflected in the CVSS AC:H component.
verifiers.exploit_sketch: 1) Attacker gains active on-path position between the SDK consumer and advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com (rogue AP, ARP/DNS spoofing, or compromised proxy). 2) Application uses the default WSBase/WSClient (base_url defaults to wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com), calls open() then subscribe(). 3) open_async() establishes the WSS connection with ssl=ssl.SSLContext(); because verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, the attacker presents any self-signed certificate for the hostname and the handshake completes without error. 4) The client sends the subscription message containing the JWT built by jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt(api_key, api_secret). 5) Attacker captures the JWT (valid ~120s) and can replay it to authenticate to Coinbase, and can read/inject account and market stream frames in both directions. Impact: theft of a valid auth token plus full read/tamper of the live data stream.

report.executive_summary: Three verified findings span two targets. The clear lead is the Coinbase Advanced Python SDK bare ssl.SSLContext() certificate-validation bypass (CWE-295, High, CVSS 7.5), already catalogued internally as PY-SSL-001 but NOT YET SUBMITTED. It is the only High-severity, high-payout item: Coinbase runs a top-tier paying HackerOne program ($200-$1,000,000 tiers) that explicitly covers open-source projects, and the finding leaks an authenticated JWT bearer to an on-path MITM. The two Weblate findings are genuine but Low-severity CWE-532 credential-in-logs/breadcrumbs issues whose impact requires existing access to logs/Sentry; Weblate's program pays modestly. Recommendation: prioritize and submit the Coinbase SSL issue now with a working MITM PoC; submit the two Weblate log-leak issues together as a single hardening report at Low.

ranked_findings.title: WebSocket TLS certificate verification disabled via bare ssl.SSLContext()
ranked_findings.exploitability: Active on-path attacker (rogue Wi-Fi/AP, ARP/DNS spoof, malicious proxy). No privilege within the victim process. Default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com so the vulnerable branch executes on every default deployment. Bare ssl.SSLContext() leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, so a self-signed cert is accepted and the authenticated JWT bearer (build_ws_jwt) is captured.
ranked_findings.why_high_value: Only High-severity item and the only one against a top-paying program. Confirmed by a 3/3 verification panel and re-confirmed in source (ssl.SSLContext() at line 150 on the primary open_async path, not gated/dead). Leaks a live authenticated JWT plus full read/inject of account and market-data frames against a crypto-exchange SDK - exactly the monetary-impact category Coinbase pays well for. ALREADY-TRACKED internally as PY-SSL-001; not yet submitted, so still actionable. Coinbase scope explicitly includes open-source projects.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Build the MITM PoC in assets/poc/ssl_mitm/: stand up a mitmproxy/self-signed-cert endpoint, point a default WSClient at it, and capture the subscribe-message JWT to prove acceptance of an invalid cert and token disclosure. Confirm the SDK is in-scope on hackerone.com/coinbase, then submit with the PoC, the one-line fix (ssl.create_default_context() or omit ssl=), and docs/findings/INSECURE_SSL_websocket_base.md. Mark report as PY-SSL-001 in the dedup store on submission.
ranked_findings.program_payout_note: Coinbase HackerOne: minimum-per-tier payouts ~ $200 to $1,000,000, internal monetary-impact risk rating, program covers all Coinbase software including open-source projects. A High CWE-295 in an official SDK leaking auth tokens is a credible mid-tier monetary payout; argue real-world impact (token replay, market-data tampering) to push the tier upward. Internal status: PY-SSL-001, already-tracked, not yet submitted.

ranked_findings.title: Authenticated push URL containing credentials written to logs at INFO level
ranked_findings.exploitability: No malicious input needed; secret leak is the impact. Requires read access to weblate.vcs logs or Sentry breadcrumbs. Org-scoped Git-provider token from *_CREDENTIALS settings is embedded as username:token@host by authenticate_url() and logged verbatim at INFO (base log() does not sanitize; sanitize_error_message is a no-op applied only to error paths). Triggers whenever a merge-request backend configures its fork remote over an http(s) push scheme.
ranked_findings.why_high_value: Cleanest of the two Weblate issues: deterministic INFO-level emission on a normal push path (not gated on SENTRY_DSN), so exposure occurs in any deployment shipping logs to aggregation. Defensible CWE-532 with a clear redaction fix and higher finder confidence (0.7) than the breadcrumb variant.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Confirm Weblate's HackerOne scope accepts logging-hygiene/CWE-532 reports and current reward posture. Write a minimal repro: configure a component with GITHUB_CREDENTIALS http(s) push and capture the INFO log line showing https://user:TOKEN@host. Propose redacting credentials in self.log()/authenticate_url before logging. Bundle with finding rank 3 into one report.
ranked_findings.program_payout_note: Weblate runs a HackerOne program but is a small open-source project historically associated with low/modest monetary rewards or swag; Low log-hygiene issues typically land at the bottom tier. Value is largely reputation/CVE credit. Program existence confirmed via search; specific reward tiers not published - reasoning partly from prior knowledge, flagged as such.

ranked_findings.title: Repository URL with embedded credentials logged to error-tracking breadcrumbs in command args
ranked_findings.exploitability: Project admin sets component.repo/push to a credentialed URL; _popen joins argv into text_cmd including the full URL and passes it to add_breadcrumb. Exposure only materializes if SENTRY_DSN is configured AND a later VCS op in the same transaction errors and fires report_error. Credentials are the admin's own and reach only the deployment's own Sentry - no cross-tenant or privilege-escalation impact.
ranked_findings.why_high_value: Real CWE-532 and a useful companion to rank 2, but lowest priority: doubly conditional (SENTRY_DSN set + a subsequent error), lowest finder confidence (0.5), and self-owned credentials reaching the operator's own Sentry. Best value is as a second data point in a single 'redact credentials before logging/breadcrumbs' hardening report.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Submit jointly with rank 2 as one consolidated credential-redaction report. Optionally add a scanner rule (CWE-532: credential-bearing URL flowing into a logging/breadcrumb sink) with paired vuln_*/safe_* fixtures under tests/fixtures/ to catch both sites and regressions. Do not open a separate disclosure for this alone.
ranked_findings.program_payout_note: Same Weblate program as rank 2; expect bottom-tier/swag or merge-as-hardening rather than a meaningful bounty. Consolidating with rank 2 avoids duplicate-triage friction and presents a stronger single narrative.

report.program_scope_notes:
Coinbase (HackerOne, hackerone.com/coinbase): Active, well-funded paying program. Public tiers list minimum-per-tier payouts spanning roughly $200 to $1,000,000 (page last updated Mar 2026), with final amounts set by an internal monetary-impact risk rating that can exceed listed minimums. Scope explicitly covers all Coinbase software including open-source projects, so coinbase-advanced-py is plausibly in scope - VERIFY the exact asset listing before submission. Highest payout potential of the three. Coinbase separately runs a $5M onchain-focused program on Cantina (not relevant to this SDK finding).

Weblate (HackerOne): A program exists (referenced on BugBountyHunter.com and HackerOne). Weblate is a small open-source/libre project; published reward tiers were not retrievable in search, and prior knowledge suggests rewards are modest-to-low (often swag, small monetary amounts, or CVE/hall-of-fame credit) - flagged as reasoned from prior knowledge, not freshly confirmed. Both Weblate findings are Low CWE-532 log/breadcrumb credential-exposure issues requiring existing log/Sentry access; payout expectations should be low. CONFIRM current scope and CWE-532 eligibility before submitting.

Chia Network (HackerOne, hackerone.com/chia_network): Confirmed active paying program (migrated from Bugcrowd to HackerOne Feb 2024). However, NONE of the three findings target Chia, so it is out of scope for this batch and carries no payout relevance here.

2026 context: HackerOne reduced some bounty rewards (The Register, May 2026); calibrate expectations slightly downward. Tooling note: WebSearch was available; exact private program reward tables on HackerOne policy pages were not directly retrievable, so specific Weblate/Chia tier figures rely partly on prior knowledge as noted.

Sources: https://hackerone.com/coinbase ; https://www.coinbase.com/blog/updating-the-coinbase-bug-bounty-program ; https://hackerone.com/chia_network ; https://www.chia.net/2024/02/29/bug-bounty-program-moving-to-hackerone-for-enhanced-security/ ; https://www.bugbountyhunter.com/program?id=weblate ; https://www.theregister.com/security/2026/05/21/hackerone-takes-an-axe-to-its-bug-bounty-rewards/5244458
recommended_priority_order: WebSocket TLS certificate verification disabled via bare ssl.SSLContext()
Authenticated push URL containing credentials written to logs at INFO level
Repository URL with embedded credentials logged to error-tracking breadcrumbs in command args

summary: Comprehensive bug-bounty security audit across 5 cloned targets: fan-out deep-read finders, adversarial 3-lens verification, ranked synthesis

phases.detail: adversarial refute-by-default panel per candidate finding

phases.title: Synthesize
phases.detail: dedup, rank by severity x exploitability x payout
